title: Resend CLI Codebase Tour
description: A guided walkthrough of the Resend CLI — a command-line interface for the Resend email API, built with TypeScript, Commander.js, and the Resend SDK.

steps.title: Welcome to Resend CLI
steps.description: Resend CLI is a developer tool for sending emails, managing domains, contacts, templates, webhooks, and more — all from the terminal. It supports interactive prompts, JSON output for scripting, and multi-profile authentication. Let's explore how it's organized.

steps.title: CLI Entry Point
steps.description: The main entry point is src/cli.ts. It uses Commander.js to define global options (api-key, profile, json, quiet), registers all command groups, and handles update checks after execution. This is where everything begins.

steps.title: Command Architecture
steps.description: Commands are organized into groups under src/commands/. Each group (emails, domains, auth, etc.) contains subcommands as individual files. There are 18+ command groups covering the full Resend API surface — from sending emails to managing webhooks and broadcasts.

steps.title: Shared Utilities
steps.description: The src/lib/ directory contains shared utilities that power the CLI experience. Key files include: client.ts (Resend SDK factory), config.ts (multi-profile credential management), actions.ts (reusable patterns for get/list/create/delete), output.ts (JSON/text formatting), and prompts.ts (interactive input via @clack/prompts).

steps.title: Authentication System
steps.description: Authentication uses a three-tier resolution: the api-key flag (highest priority), RESEND_API_KEY environment variable, or stored credentials in config/resend/credentials.json. The auth commands handle login (browser-based or manual), logout, profile switching, and multi-account support.

steps.title: Test Suite
steps.description: Tests use Vitest and mirror the source structure under tests/. Unit tests cover individual commands and utilities, while tests/e2e/ contains smoke tests with a 60-second timeout. The tests/helpers.ts file provides shared mocks for exit codes, SDK errors, TTY simulation, and output capture.

steps.title: CI/CD & Distribution
steps.description: GitHub Actions workflows handle testing, binary releases, and installation verification across platforms. The CLI is distributed via npm, Homebrew, cURL/PowerShell installers, and standalone binaries built with yao-pkg.

steps.title: The Full Picture
steps.description: You've seen how Resend CLI is structured: the commander.js entry point (src/cli.ts) delegates to command groups (src/commands/), powered by shared utilities (src/lib/) for authentication, output formatting, and interactive UX. Tests mirror the source, and CI handles multi-platform distribution. Happy exploring!
